title: Generalizations of Ohta's theorem for holomorphic modular forms on certain Shimura varieties
speaker: Marco Sangiovanni
abstract: Ohta described the ordinary part of the 'etale cohomology of towers of modular curves in terms of Hida families. Ohta's approach crucially depended on the one-dimensional nature of modular curves. In this talk, I will present joint work with Chris Skinner which generalizes Ohta's results to other Shimura varieties; I will focus on the case of Hilbert modular varieties. This generalization is expected to have many applications for the construction of Euler systems. Our approach is based on an idea of Faltings, clarified for us by B. Bhatt under the framework of integral p-adic Hodge theory, as developed by Bhatt--Morrow--Scholze, Bhatt--Scholze and Bhatt--Lurie

proof_stages.label: Classical Ohta theorem on the ordinary modular-curve tower
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the tower of modular curves X_1(Np^r), inverse-limit ordinary étale homology with Hecke and diamond actions, and states Ohta's two-step Galois-equivariant filtration identifying the plus and minus pieces with Hida ordinary Hecke/modular-form objects.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic motivation for an Ohta-type description
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that understanding the ordinary piece of completed cohomology gives access to ordinary Galois deformation theory and to Euler-system constructions in p-adic families, motivating analogous results for higher-dimensional Shimura varieties.

proof_stages.label: Replacement of the Jacobian connected-étale argument
proof_stages.summary: Analyzing Ohta's proof shows that its key use of the connected-étale sequence for Jacobians is intrinsically one-dimensional, so the proposed higher-dimensional substitute is an integral Hodge--Tate decomposition for middle-degree cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert modular analogue and theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: For a totally real field F of degree d, the speaker defines the Hilbert modular tower with Gamma_1(Np^r)-level, its ordinary middle-degree interior homology, and states the main theorem: ordinary cusp forms map injectively, and after localization at a good Hida maximal ideal isomorphically, to the appropriate inertia-coinvariant Galois-invariant part.

proof_stages.label: Integral Hodge--Tate/prismatic complex and splitting
proof_stages.summary: Using the Bhatt--Morrow--Scholze A_inf complex, the Hodge--Tate complex is defined by reduction along theta, and Bhatt--Lurie's integral splitting for p larger than the dimension supplies the substitute for the classical connected-étale decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Comparison maps from Hodge--Tate differentials to étale cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Faltings's primitive comparison theorem and its extension to finite, possibly nonsmooth covers give maps from Hodge--Tate pieces, expressed via pullbacks of differentials from a smooth model, into integral p-adic étale cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary control and integral modular-form lattices over the tower
proof_stages.summary: The comparison map is applied to the Shimura tower by taking a hyperspecial smooth model and its Gamma_1(p^r)-normalizations, comparing the resulting étale lattice with q-expansion-integral ordinary forms; controlling off-middle ordinary cohomology and localizing at good maximal ideals yields the asserted Ohta-type result, with stronger control in Hilbert-surface cases.

examples.name: Rankin-convolution Euler system in families
examples.note: The Rankin-convolution Euler system of Kings--Loeffler--Zerbes illustrates how Ohta-type local descriptions of ordinary cohomology support explicit reciprocity laws for families.
examples.engagement_reason: The Rankin-convolution Euler system is cited as a motivation/example of where Ohta-type results are used, but no construction or calculation is carried out on it.

examples.name: Kato modular units and Eisenstein-series classes
examples.note: Kato's modular-unit construction, contrasted with Eisenstein-series classes on more general Shimura varieties, illustrates why analogous integral cohomology classes are desirable beyond modular curves.
examples.engagement_reason: Kato’s modular units and Eisenstein-series classes are described as sources of Euler-system classes, but the speaker does not work through a specific modular unit or Eisenstein class.

examples.name: Theta-series Hida family over an imaginary quadratic field
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker uses a theta series with p split in the associated imaginary quadratic field to show concretely how the Ohta filtration can yield a family of cohomology classes.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces how a theta series with p split gives a Hida-family element, maps via Ohta’s isomorphism to a class, and uses splitting of the extension to obtain a family of cohomology classes.
